narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 36, you've built a respectable academic foundation that stands out in several ways. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend law school full-time, which is the norm for about 93% of students in the dataset we used. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' profiles to predict bar exam success on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4, which is below the typical 5.53 average and pushed the model toward predicting failure, signaling relatively weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, as it's stronger than the 3.21 average and pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Similarly, your LSAT score of 36, while slightly under the 36.70 average, also contributed positively by nudging the odds toward bar exam success. Overall, that dip in your later-year school rankings outweighed your other strengths, making your profile riskier than most admitted students whose trajectories showed consistent or improving performance. You're not far off—your GPA and LSAT put you ahead of typical applicants in those areas—but the model prioritizes patterns linked to first-time bar passage. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting late-stage academic deciles through targeted coursework or grad school performance, retake the LSAT if feasible to hit or exceed 37, and gather strong letters highlighting your upward momentum. Consider part-time programs or bridge courses that demonstrate bar prep readiness, and reapply after showcasing recent achievements to shift that probability below the threshold. With your baseline strengths, targeted improvements can make a real difference.
